query: Angiofibroma classification in relation to paranasal sinus involvement

retrieved_docs.title: Nasopharyngeal Angiofibroma -- Staging
retrieved_docs.content: Stage I disease is confined to the nasal cavity and nasopharynx. Stage II involves the paranasal sinuses and lateral pterygopalatine fossa without residual vascularity. Stage III and IV disease are defined by skull base erosion with involvement of the orbit and infratemporal fossa, distinguished by the presence of ICA vascular supply in stage IV. Stage V disease is subdivided according to laterality, with medial [stage V(M)] versus lateral [stage V(L)] intracranial extension and associated residual vascularity.

retrieved_docs.title: Nasopharyngeal Angiofibroma -- Staging
retrieved_docs.content: Other proposed classifications are less commonly used or remain too new to have their effectiveness relative to established systems assessed. Examples include the Sessions, Chandler, and Instituto Nacional de Cancerología (INCan) staging methods. The most recent classification was introduced in 2010 by a group from the University of Pittsburgh Medical Center, based on endoscopic staging. [52] This system classifies JNA into 5 stages, with greater emphasis on residual vascularity, primarily from the ICA after preoperative embolization. Tumor size and paranasal sinus invasion are deemphasized, as they are considered limited predictors of outcome after resection.

retrieved_docs.title: Nasopharyngeal Angiofibroma -- Staging
retrieved_docs.content: The Radkowski system is divided into 3 stages, each with subcategories. Stage Ia disease is confined to the nasal cavity and nasopharyngeal vault, whereas extension into the paranasal sinuses defines stage Ib. Stage II disease is characterized by extension into the pterygopalatine fossa. Stage IIa is marked by minimal extension, stage IIb comprises complete fossa involvement, and stage IIc is characterized by involvement of the infratemporal fossa or posterior to the pterygoid plates. Stage III disease reflects advanced growth affecting the skull base and intracranial structures. Stage IIIa is defined by minimal extension into the middle cranial fossa or pterygoid plates, while stage IIIb indicates intracranial extension with or without cavernous sinus invasion. [50]

retrieved_docs.title: Nasopharyngeal Angiofibroma -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Though histologically benign, JNA often demonstrates aggressive features with local invasion into the nasal turbinates, nasal septum, and medial pterygoid lamina. The tumor commonly extends into the nasal cavity, nasopharynx, and pterygopalatine fossa, with larger lesions involving the sphenoid, maxillary, and ethmoid sinuses. Growth may also extend through the inferior orbital fissure and into the masticator space via the infratemporal fossa. Severe disease may involve the orbit or intracranial structures, reported in approximately 10% to 37% of cases. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Nasal Cavity -- Surgical Considerations -- Juvenile Nasopharyngeal Angiofibroma (JNA)
retrieved_docs.content: JNA is the most common vascular mass found in the nasal cavity. It is typically seen in adolescent males with recurrent epistaxis and even nasal obstruction. The site it is encountered most often is the roof of the nasal cavity near the sphenopalatine foramen. This tumor is locally aggressive but it does not metastasize. To diagnose, imaging is the preferred method; a biopsy is contraindicated due to the risk of hemorrhage. To treat JNA, the surgeon can choose between endoscopic surgery, open surgical approach, chemotherapy, and radiation, or hormonal therapy. The Fisch Classification of invasion aids in determining which treatment modality to use. [30] [31] Fisch's Classification of Invasion: Only in the nasal cavity Pterygomaxillary fossa or sinuses Infratemporal fossa, orbit, or parasellar region Cavernous sinus, optic chiasm area, or pituitary fossa Other indications for nasal surgery include cancers, nasal valve collapse, and cosmetics.

retrieved_docs.title: Nasopharyngeal Angiofibroma -- Staging
retrieved_docs.content: Several methods have been proposed for staging and classifying nasopharyngeal angiofibroma to guide surgical decision-making and adjunctive treatment. The Radkowski and Andrews-Fisch systems are the most widely used, as both correlate well with surgical approach, recurrence, and outcome. The University of Pittsburgh Medical Center classification was introduced more recently, emphasizing endoscopic staging in line with contemporary surgical techniques. All systems primarily determine stage based on anatomic extent.

retrieved_docs.title: Nasopharyngeal Angiofibroma -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Contrast-enhanced CT typically demonstrates an avidly enhancing soft tissue mass within the posterior nasal cavity near the sphenopalatine foramen, often extending into the nasopharynx, pterygopalatine fossa, and adjacent sinuses. [32] Air cells may be evident, depending on tumor size, opacification of the sphenoid sinus, maxillary sinus, or ethmoid, either from obstruction or direct infiltration.

retrieved_docs.title: Nasopharyngeal Angiofibroma -- Staging
retrieved_docs.content: The Andrews-Fisch system is similar, with an extra stage delineating the intracranial extent. Stage I is limited to the nasal cavity and nasopharyngeal vault. Stage II includes pterygopalatine fossa or any sinus invasion. Stage IIIa involves extension into the infratemporal fossa or orbital invasion, whereas stage IIIb describes intracranial, extradural extension into the parasellar region. Intradural extension denotes a more advanced stage IV disease, with cavernous sinus, pituitary fossa, or optic chiasm invasion separating stage IVa from stage IVb. [51]

retrieved_docs.title: Nasopharyngeal Angiofibroma -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The earliest known documentation of nasopharyngeal angiofibroma dates to Hippocrates in the 5th century B.C. [1] The condition is also known as juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma (JNA), juvenile angiofibroma, or fibromatous or angiofibromatous hamartoma of the nasal cavity. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Nasopharyngeal Angiofibroma -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The term "nasopharyngeal" may not be entirely accurate. Some sources state that the tumor arises from the sphenopalatine foramen and posterior nasal cavity, while others suggest a more choanal and nasopharyngeal origin. [3] [4] Research consistently agrees, however, that JNA is a benign, highly vascular lesion comprising approximately 0.05% to 0.5% of all head and neck masses. [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Nasopharyngeal Angiofibroma -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Sinonasal polyps are inflammatory lesions that can become hypervascular after repeated injury but have less vascularity than JNA. The most common form is the antrochoanal polyp, which originates in the maxillary sinus and extends through the maxillary ostium into the nasal cavity. Sinonasal polyps may also arise from or extend into the nasopharynx, although they typically do not reach the sphenopalatine foramen or pterygopalatine fossa. Osseous remodeling in this condition is smooth rather than destructive. Imaging demonstrates peripheral enhancement without central enhancement, which differs from the avid enhancement pattern of nasopharyngeal angiofibroma. Similar to JNA, sinonasal polyps may occur in adolescents or young adults and present with nasal obstruction, but they rarely cause epistaxis.

retrieved_docs.title: Nasopharyngeal Angiofibroma -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Nasopharyngeal angiofibroma is a rare, benign, highly vascular tumor of the posterior nasal cavity, comprising approximately 0.05% to 0.5% of all head and neck masses. The condition occurs almost exclusively in male adolescents and was first surgically addressed by Hippocrates, who described a “longitudinal splitting of the ridge of the nose.” Histologically benign, the tumor demonstrates locally aggressive behavior with invasion into the nasal turbinates, nasal septum, and medial pterygoid lamina, frequently extending into the nasal cavity, nasopharynx, and pterygopalatine fossa. Large lesions may involve the paranasal sinuses or intracranial structures.

retrieved_docs.title: Nasopharyngeal Angiofibroma -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Bone kernel postprocessing aids in evaluating osseous remodeling or destruction. Expansion of the nasal cavity and pterygopalatine fossa, as well as anterior bowing of the posterior maxillary sinus wall (Holman-Miller sign), may be secondary to mass effect. [33] Osseous destruction is more common in aggressive tumors, particularly those involving the skull base with intracranial extension.

retrieved_docs.title: Nasopharyngeal Angiofibroma -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: As with CT, MRI demonstrates intense enhancement following gadolinium administration. MRI is also particularly useful for evaluating tumor extension into the cavernous or sphenoid sinus or identifying perineural spread through the skull base or into the orbit. [36] Magnetic resonance angiography, similar to CT angiography, can show enlargement of the ipsilateral ECA or internal maxillary artery, although it is less effective in defining the vascular source.

retrieved_docs.title: Nasopharyngeal Angiofibroma -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Traditional cerebral angiography remains useful, especially when multiple feeding arteries are present. Selective catheterization of the ICA and ECA, as well as subselecting individual branches, can guide preoperative decision-making.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Pterygopalatine Fossa -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: This region is critical in transmitting parasympathetic fibers and blood vessels to and from the face and is clinically important in disease spread and cranial surgical approaches. A 3-dimensional understanding of the pterygopalatine fossa is crucial for the rhinologist and basicranial surgeon to control epistaxis and remove basicranial lesions. A pterygopalatine space approach is used to treat lesions in the areas posterior to the maxillary sinus, including the pterygopalatine space and lateral sphenoid recess. Pathologic processes in this space are rare, with the most common being juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma, neurogenic tumors, sinonasal malignancy perineural extension, and meningoencephaloceles. [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 277
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose are associated with nasal disorder (اِضطِراب أنفي).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1981
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose are medical interventions that address nasal disorders (اِضطِراب أنفي). Within this category, control of epistaxis is identified as a specific type of Procedure on Nose, indicating that managing nosebleeds is a subset of nasal procedural care. This relationship highlights that interventions aimed at controlling epistaxis are directly linked to the broader management of nasal disorders, emphasizing the role of targeted procedural treatment in the evaluation and care of patients with nasal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1174
retrieved_docs.content: Epistaxis (nosebleed) is classified as a specific type of nasal disorder that falls under the broader category of “nasal disorders, not classified elsewhere,” indicating that it is considered a child condition within this unspecified nasal disorder classification.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2578
retrieved_docs.content: Other upper respiratory disease is categorized under the broader group of nasal disorders not elsewhere classified (اضطرابات أنفية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر). Within this same nasal‑disorder category, epistaxis (الرُعاف) is identified as a specific condition. This hierarchical organization shows that both the general classification of other upper respiratory diseases and the particular manifestation of nosebleeds are subsumed by the overarching nasal disorder category, facilitating systematic diagnosis, documentation, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 235
retrieved_docs.content: أنفية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر also has الرُعاف as a child concept, reinforcing the connection between unspecified nasal disorders and bleeding from the nose. Overall, these relationships illustrate a hierarchical and functional network linking nasal‑septum disorders, their classification within upper‑respiratory disease taxonomy, and the corresponding diagnostic and therapeutic procedures—particularly those aimed at controlling epistaxis—thereby providing a comprehensive framework for understanding and managing nasal‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2936
retrieved_docs.content: الرُعاف (epistaxis) is classified as a child entity within اضطرابات أنفية، غير مصنف في موضع آخر (nasal disorders, not classified elsewhere), indicating that it is a specific type of nasal disorder that does not fit into other subcategories. Clinical attention to this condition centers on the control of epistaxis, which is identified as a primary focus of management for patients experiencing nasal bleeding. Recognizing الرُعاف as a distinct subset of unclassified nasal disorders facilitates targeted therapeutic strategies aimed at effectively controlling bleeding and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2411
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of nasal bone is described as having the morphology of ورم خبيث أولي (primary malignant tumor). In addition, سرطانة توتية is classified as a type of ورم خبيث أولي, indicating that it too represents a primary malignant tumor. Both the nasal bone neoplasm and سرطانة توتية therefore share the underlying categorization of primary malignant tumor, reflecting their common oncologic identity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2192
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of bone of the face is a specific type of neoplasm of bone of the face, representing a malignant tumor that originates in the facial bones. This relationship indicates that the broader category “neoplasm of bone of the face” encompasses the more specific entity “primary malignant neoplasm of bone of the face,” clarifying the hierarchical classification of facial bone tumors for diagnostic and therapeutic considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 1 -- Community 248
retrieved_docs.content: inverse_isa of سرطان الرأس والعنق, highlighting its role as a specific manifestation of head‑and‑neck cancer. Together, these interrelated entities illustrate that malignant bone tumors of the skull and facial region are integrated within broader head and neck oncology, can arise as primary or metastatic lesions, and are represented across multiple linguistic and classification frameworks, which is essential for comprehensive diagnosis, coding, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2556
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are both examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, representing pathological processes in which cancerous cells originate and grow aggressively at their respective anatomical sites. As primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, each condition reflects a direct, localized malignant transformation of tissue—one affecting the nasal bone and the other affecting the thymus. Recognizing that both entities share the same fundamental nature as pathological processes of primary malignant neoplastic growth aids clinicians in understanding their aggressive behavior, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing treatment strategies tailored to the specific organ involved.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1978
retrieved_docs.content: Anterior epistaxis is classified as اضطراب في الحاجز الأنفي (a disorder of the nasal septum). This nasal‑septum disorder is mapped to the category “other upper respiratory disease,” which in turn is placed under the broader “nos” (not otherwise specified) parent classification. This hierarchical relationship situates anterior epistaxis within the larger framework of upper respiratory conditions, linking a specific type of nosebleed to a general respiratory disease group and ultimately to a nonspecific diagnostic category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 211
retrieved_docs.content: nasal endoscopy with maxillary antrostomy is placed in the group of diagnostic procedures that examine the nose, mouth and pharynx. Collectively, these entities and relationships form an “is‑a,” “has‑child,” “has‑parent,” and “method‑of” taxonomy that supports precise clinical classification, interdisciplinary communication, accurate coding, and informed procedural selection in the management of urological, renal, female‑perineal, gastrointestinal and related diagnostic‑therapeutic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1929
retrieved_docs.content: Nasal endoscopy with maxillary antrostomy is classified as a diagnostic procedure involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx, providing clinicians with direct endoscopic visualization and access to the maxillary sinus for assessment of sinonasal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2680
retrieved_docs.content: Anterior epistaxis is identified as a disorder of the nasal septum (اضطراب في الحاجز الأنفي), representing bleeding that originates from the front portion of the nasal cavity and is commonly associated with structural or mucosal abnormalities of the septum.
